How it works

No lockups · Principal never plays · Draws provable on-chain

SCROLL
MEGA (DEMO) · 1,418 SOL
draw in 0:26
×
THE PROBLEM WITH 7%
Staking 25 SOL pays you about $0.37 a day. Feel anything?

Seven percent a year is good math and terrible television. VaultDrop routes the same yield you already earn into something with a pulse: every week, your balance is a stack of tickets in a draw you can verify. Win, and it's real SOL in your wallet. Don't, and you've lost exactly nothing — your deposit never left your reach.

STAKING
25 SOL → +0.0048 SOL ($0.37) · every day · forever
certain. silent.
VAULTDROP
52 draws a year + one rolling Mega Vault
same yield. different physics.
See your odds →
How it works
01

Deposit SOL. Get jpSOL — same value, always redeemable.

02

Holding is your ticket. Weight = your balance × time. No lockups.

03

Win from the yield. Or withdraw everything, anytime. Principal never plays.

Two futures for the same deposit

IF THE VAULT HOLDS
25,000 SOLepoch-1 scale
100,000 SOL
500,000 SOL

Small vault: you win often, for less. Big vault: you win rarely, for life-changing money. Same expected value either way — they're just different games.

Right now the vault is empty. At epoch-1 scale your 100 SOL is a big fish in a small pond — a shot roughly every 13 Sundays instead of every 50. That version of the game only exists once.

WHAT IT ACTUALLY COSTS YOU
Staking pays you
7 SOL
$539
VaultDrop routes to prizes← your expected take
6.3 SOL
$485
The difference — that's the fee
−0.7 SOL
−$54

$1.04 a week is the whole price of the game. It buys 52 shots at $82 and ~2 shots at $18,202. Your $7,705 never moves, and you can withdraw it any time.

No amount of 7% will ever change your life — linear yield can't produce a life-changing outcome at any horizon. A skewed payout is the only mechanism that turns small savings into large sums, and here the ticket doesn't cost you the deposit.

Over time, expected value ≈ the yield you route in, minus the protocol fee. What changes is the shape: instead of a drip, you hold a shot.

Illustrative — final protocol parameters (APY feed, fee, winner count, Mega share) are confirmed at launch. Odds share assumes a steady balance held the full week. Dollar figures use the live Pyth SOL/USD price shown above and hide when the feed is unavailable.

How our draws are verified +
What can go wrong (and what can't)
CAN'T
Your principal funding prizes. The program can only move yield into the prize escrow — deposits are structurally out of its reach. The invariant is one line of math, published in the docs.
Being locked in. jpSOL redeems at the program rate any time. No lockups, no exit fee, no permission.
CAN — AND WHAT WE DO ABOUT IT
Smart-contract bugs. The programs are open source; audit status will be published on this page before deposits open.
JitoSOL exposure. Deposits are held as JitoSOL, a liquid-staked SOL token — its own contract and stake-pool risk exist. If the stake pool ever took a loss, withdrawals switch to a uniform pro-rata rate: everyone exits at the same rate, order doesn't matter.
VRF liveness. If randomness is delayed, the draw is delayed. Funds are unaffected — withdrawals keep working.
Upgrade authority. Who holds it, and under what multisig, is disclosed here before launch.
Questions
Is my deposit ever at risk?
+
No. Prizes are funded entirely from staking yield. The program can only move yield into the prize escrow — your principal is not able to fund prizes. You can withdraw your full balance at any time. For the full picture of what can and can't go wrong, see 'What can go wrong' above.
What can I actually win?
+
What happens if I never win?
+
Can whales win everything?
+
Why balance × time?
+

+

+

+
Where is VaultDrop available?
+
FOR BUILDERS
jpSOL

Under the hood: deposits mint jpSOL, a SOL-value-stable share token. 1 jpSOL redeems for its SOL value at the program rate, any time — draw exposure rides along for free.

invariant: sum(jpSOL supply × rate) ≤ vault assets · deposits mint at rate · withdrawals burn at rate · yield never mints shares
